label: ai
topic: A lifestyle app that tells people what to do gains cultish popularity
text:
The first time I heard about Luminos was at our Tuesday coffee, when Sarah showed me her phone screen like she was revealing a secret.

"It told me to make shakshuka last night," she said, with the particular reverence people usually reserve for personal trainers or therapists. "And it was incredible. Like, life-changingly good shakshuka."

"An app told you to make eggs," I said.

"It's not just an app. It's a whole system. It learns what you need."

I looked at Rachel, hoping for the usual alliance of scepticism we maintained against Sarah's enthusiasms, but Rachel was nodding slowly in the way that meant she had already downloaded it and was waiting for a natural moment to mention this.

"You too?" I said.

"It's actually really good," Rachel said, not meeting my eyes. "It suggested I take a different route to work and I found this bakery I'd walked past a thousand times without noticing."

"So you're both taking orders from your phones now. That's fine. That's totally normal behaviour."

"It's not orders," Sarah said. "It's suggestions. Personalised suggestions based on your patterns and goals."

"What goals did you enter?"

"You don't enter goals. It figures them out."

This should have been the end of it. I should have finished my latte, made a joke about our imminent robot overlords, and gone back to my desk. But the thing about Luminos was that once you noticed it, it was everywhere. The barista at the coffee shop mentioned it. My neighbour was rearranging her living room because Luminos had told her the feng shui was suboptimal. A stranger on the bus was reading a book she described to her companion as a Luminos recommendation, and the companion was nodding like this was a perfectly sensible way to choose literature.

At work, the first sign was Marcus in accounting putting a plant on his desk. Marcus had never expressed interest in any living thing, including his colleagues. Then Elena in marketing started leaving the office at exactly five-fifteen every day, which was unprecedented. When I asked her about it, she smiled with the serene expression of someone who has recently joined a wellness cult and said, "Luminos says my cortisol spikes if I stay past five."

"Your cortisol," I repeated.

"It integrates with my smartwatch. It knows."

The real tipping point came when my manager, David, called a team meeting to discuss what he called "holistic workflow optimisation" but which turned out to be a twenty-minute pitch for Luminos. He had prepared slides. He showed us graphs of his sleep quality and step count and something he called his "intentionality score," which I suspected Luminos had invented specifically to give David something to optimise.

"It's completely optional," David said, in the tone managers use when something is not optional.

"What if we don't want an app managing our lives?" I asked.

David looked at me with genuine confusion, as if I had asked what if we don't want oxygen. "Why wouldn't you?" he said. "It's free for the first month."

I held out for three more weeks. During those weeks, Sarah lost four pounds, Rachel finished a novel she'd been stalling on for two years, Marcus got a promotion, and Elena started bringing homemade sourdough to the office. Everyone around me was becoming measurably, visibly better at being a person, and I was still eating cereal at ten p.m. and doom-scrolling until my eyes ached.

I downloaded it on a Thursday. The setup took eleven minutes. I answered no questions. It didn't ask any.

The first notification arrived at six-forty-seven the next morning: Go for a walk before you check your email.

I put on my shoes. Outside, the air smelled like rain and cut grass. Two blocks from my apartment, I passed a woman walking her dog who smiled at me for no reason. I smiled back, and for a moment I forgot that I was doing this because a piece of software had told me to.

That evening, Luminos told me to call my mother. I hadn't called her in three weeks. She was surprised and pleased, and we talked for forty minutes about nothing in particular, and when I hung up I felt something I couldn't quite name, something warm and slightly unsettling, like being cared for by a stranger who somehow knew exactly what you needed.
